text: ALLAMPS, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, arrond. of Toul, 4 m. WNW of Colombey. Pop. 451. Glass is manufactured here.

ALLAN, a river of Perthshire, flowing in a SE direction from the braes of Ogilvie to Dunblane, where it turns S, skirts the v. known as the Bridge-of-Allan, and joins the Forth 2 m. above Stirling. Its entire course is about 18 m. The Scottish Central railway runs through the most picturesque portion of the valley of the A.—Also a small tributary of the Teviot, descending from Skelfhill pen, and flowing into that river 3 m. above Hawick.—Also a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Drome, 6 m. E by S of Montelimart. Pop. 1,124. Excellent red wines are grown here.—The name in British topography is more frequently written ALAN and ALLEN.

ALLAN (BRIDGE OF), a v. of Perthshire, 3 m. N of Stirling, on the post-road from Stirling to Callandar, and a station of the Scottish Central railway. It is a rising place in consequence of its proximity to the mineral well of Airthrie.
ALLAN (MOUNT), a hill in SE Australia, a little to the N of the Goobang creek, a branch of the Lachlan, in S lat. 33° 9'.

ALLANCHE, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Cantal, and arrond. of Murat. The cant. comprising 12 com. had a pop. of 10,809 in 1831. The com. and v. which are situated on a stream of the same name, a branch of the Alagnon, 10 m. NNE of Murat, had a pop. in 1842 of 2,605.
ALLAND, a town of Austria, in the circle of the lower Ens, to the W of Heiligen Kreuz. Pop. 751.
ALLARIZ, a pleasant town of Spain, in Galicia, on the Arnoya river, 12 m SSE of Orense. Pop. 1,500.
ALLARMONT, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Vosges, arrond. of Saint-Dié. Pop. 754.

ALLAS (STRAIT OF), a channel through the Sunda chain of islands, formed by the E coast of the island of Lombok, and the W coast of that of Sumbawa, of which the central part is in S lat. 8° 30', E. long. 116° 30'. It extends about 46 m. in a NNE and SSW direction, and contracts to 6 m. in breadth James-river on the E. These rivers rise in small ALL to any of the straits E of Java, the tides being moderate, with soundings along the Lombok side, which is also low land, and affords ample supplies from the plantations and villages which are scattered over it. It receives its name from the town of A., at the western extremity of Sumbawa.—Horsburgh.—Jukes.
ALLASSAC, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Corréze, arrond. and 8 m. NE of Brives. Pop. 4,049.

ALLAUCH, a com. and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, arrond. and 6 m. E by N of Marseilles. Pop. 3,711. Excellent white wines are made here. It is near the source of the Jaret. Alt. above sea-level 793 ft.
ALLAYARTAKANDA, or ULYARTANDO, a town of Sinde, 20 m. E of Hyderabad, in N lat. 25° 21', E long. 68° 41'. Pop. 5,000. It has some trade, and manufactures of cotton.
ALLCANNINGS, a parish in Wiltshire, 4 m. E by N of Devizes. Area 4,470 acres. Pop. 3,160.

ALLE, a river of East Prussia, in the circle of Königsberg, flowing into the Pregel, on the I. bank, at Wehlau, after a course of 136 m. in a general NE direction, in which it passes Allenstein, Gutstadt, Heilsberg, Schippenheil, Friedland, and Allenburg. It is navigable to Friedland.—Also a river of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, an affluent of the Doubs, on which a Catholic v. of the same name is situated.
ALLEEAHABAD. See ALIABAD.
ALLE'E BLANCHE, an elevated valley in the Pennine Alps, lying to the SE of Mont Blanc, intersected by the Doire flowing from the Lac de Combul in a NE direction.
ALLEGAN, a co. of Michigan, U.S., on Lake Michigan. Area 840 sq. m. Pop. 1,783. Its capital of the same name is built on both sides of the Kala mazu river, 161 m. W by N of Detroit. Pop. 634.

ALLEGHANY, or Appallachian Mountains, a great mountain-chain in N. America, which extending in a general direction from SW to NE, from the northern confines of Georgia and Alabama, in N lat. 34°, and terminating in a series of granitic mountainranges on the southern shores of the St. Lawrence, has a total length of 1,200 m., with a varying breadth of from 90 to 150 m. It is called Alleghany N of the Potomac, and Appallachian S of that river. This chain, consisting of a succession of nearly parallel ridges, bearing different names, and running in their greater extent nearly parallel to the coast of the Atlantic, and at a distance of from 50 to 130 m. from it, but retiring farther into the interior as they proceed S, irregularly intersected by rivers and separated by generally narrow vallies with "basins, gaps, and even peaks innumerable" [Rafinesque], is divided into two principal ranges,—the Alleghany mountains, properly so called, and the Blue mountains. The former, which is the more westerly, preserves throughout its entire course a distance of from 240 to 300 m. from the Atlantic, and a nearly uniform elevation of 3,000 ft. above sea-level. Its rise commences near the great bend of the Tenessee river, in the S of Alabama; whence it runs in a NE direction, till it reaches the northern frontiers of Virginia, when it turns towards the NW. In Pennsylvania, it resumes a NE course, and spreads over all the central part of that state. Near the source of the southern branch of the Susquehanna it divides into two ramifications, the principal of which bends in an easterly direction, and after crossing the Susquehanna terminates in the mountains of Cats kill, one of the highest summits of the northern Alleghany range. The principal rivers which issue from this chain, are the Gohiogany, the Monongahela, the Greenbriar, Kanhava, and Cumberland, on the W; and the Delaware, the Susquehanna, Potomac, and streams which, after creeping silently and quietly along the bases of the mountains, by many and intricate windings, gather themselves at length into rivers. The eastern chain, called the Blue ridge or Blue mountains, is considerably narrower than the Alleghany; but runs in a generally parallel direction. It passes off from a culminating point on the western range in N Carolina called the Black mountain, 6,476 ft. high, and bifurcates in Virginia, sending off one branch through the NE states as far as Quebec, and another to the W as far as Kingston in Upper Canada. The low country between these two branches, called the Appallachian valley, is composed of the older limestones. From Upper Canada the western bifurcation ranges round to the NW and joins the Rocky mountains. The average height of the Blue mountains is about 1,500 ft., rising from a base not exceeding 9 m. in breadth. Their highest point seems to be White top, in the Iron mountains, which is said to have an alt. of 6,000 ft.; Powell's in the most westerly ridge has an alt. of 4,500 ft. The most important rivers which take their rise in the Blue mountains are the Roanoke, the Pedee, Santee, Savannah, and Alatamaha on the E; the Alabama and the Mobile on the S; and on the W the Tenessee. The beautiful and fertile valley which separates the two great divisions of the Alleghany chain is intersected by several ramifications, of which the principal are the Northern and Southern mountains of Virginia. The Laurel mountains lie on the W of the Alleghany, and run in a parallel direction to that range. The principal ramifications to the E of the Hudson are the Green mountains, with their adjunct the Taghconnue, and Mount Tom, which rise near Newhaven in Connecticut. The former intersects Vermont, and extends in isolated summits to the extremity of Cape Gaspe; the latter runs through N. Hampshire into Canada. To the E of Mount Tom are the White mountains, a group of dome-shaped summits, the highest of which, Mount Washington, rises 6,630 ft. above the level of the sea, and is the most elevated of the Alleghany chain. All of these ramifications are distinguished from the ridges commonly called Appallachian, to the S and W of the Hudson, by their line of direction being parallel to that of the principal water-courses, and perpendicular to the coast. These ridges rarely present naked summits, but are generally wooded to the top. They consist of granite, gneiss, mica and clay slate, and primitive limestone. The loftier portion in the N. is composed chiefly of primitive rocks, and contains vast stores of iron, with valuable marbles. The country intersected by this vast range, Rafinesque supposes to have been "once a region of lakes, which have burst their barriers and left many large basins."

ALLEGHANY, a river of the United States of America, which rises on the W side of the Alleghany mountains, about 30 m. E of Lake Erie, near the northern confines of Pennsylvania. Its general direction is at first NE, until it enters the state of New York, when it bends to the NW, and, after a brief circuit, returns to Pennsylvania. It then pursues its course with numerous sinuosities towards the S, receiving on its right the Conewago, which conveys the waters of Lake Chalangue and French Creek, which forms a direct communication with Canada, and on the left Toby's Creek and the Conemaugh. At Pittsburg, in N lat. 40° 28′, W long. 80° 8′, at an elev. of 1,138 ft. above tide-water in the Atlantic, it unites with the Monongahela, and forms the Ohio, here a quarter of a mile broad. The entire length of the course of the A. is 400 m. It is navigable as far as Hamilton, 270 m. above its junction with the Ohio. Its banks are generally fertile, presenting tracts of fine cultivation and meadow-land, interspersed with ridges covered ALL with white oak and chestnut; and its waters are remarkable for their limpidness.

ALLEGHANY, a co. of New York, U. S., on the Genesee river, which intersects it from S to N. Area 1,185 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 40,975. The E and W parts of the co. consist of an elevated table-land, from 1,500 to 2,500 ft. above tide-water in the Hudson. The Genesee valley canal, commencing at Olean, on the Alleghany river, passes through the county from S to N, and forms a communication from the Erie canal at Rochester to Pittsburg.-Also a co. in Pennsylvania, in which the Monongahela and Alleghany rivers unite to form the Ohio, which runs 14 m. within the co. Pop. in 1840, 81,235. The district though hilly is fertile, and the natural growth is dense and large. The town of A. on the NW side of the A. river, may be regarded as a suburb of Pittsburg, and has a pop. of 10,089. Pittsburg is the cap.—Also a co. in the extreme W of Maryland. The Alleghany ridge in passing through this co. divides the waters of the Youhioginy flowing NW into the Monongahela, from the head-branch of the Potomac flowing SW toward the Atlantic. The great Baltimore and Ohio railroad commences at Cumberland, the cap. of this co. Pop. in 1840, 15,690.—Also a co. in Virginia, drained by the headwaters of James river. Area 521 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 1,839. — Also a township in Armstrong co. Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,839. — Also a township in Huntingdon co. in the same state, on the Beaverdam river. Pop. 2,225. - There are several other townships of the same name in the state of Pennsylvania: in Venango co. with a pop. of 804; in Somerset co. with a pop. of 633; in Westmoreland co. with a pop. of 2,641; and in Cambria co. with a pop. of 1,217.

ALLEGRANZA, the northernmost of the Canary isles; in N lat. 29° 25½, W long. 13° 30′ 45″; 7 m. N of Graciosa. It is a mass of lava and cinders, rising to an alt. of 939 ft. The only landing-place is on the S side. About 40 persons find employment on the island in collecting orchilla.

ALLEGRE, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire. The cant. comprises 7 communes, with a pop. in 1821 of 8,171. The com. and v. are in the arrond. and 16 m. NW of Le Puy. Pop. 2,033.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Gard, arrond. of Alais. Pop. 1,020.—See also Alegre.
ALLEINS, a com. of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, arrond. of Arles, cant. of Eyguieres. Pop. 1,261.
ALLEMAGNE, a com. of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Caen. Pop. 976.
ALLEMAND-ROMBACH, a com. of France, in the dep. of Haut Rhin, arrond. of Colmar. Pop. 1,992.
ALLEMANS, a com. of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Riberac. Pop. 1,423.
ALLEMANS (LES), a com. of France, in the dep. of Ariège, arrond. of Pamiers. Pop. 891.
ALLEMOND-EN-OYSANS, a com. of France, in the dep. of Isère, arrond. of Grenoble, cant. of Bourg d'Oysans, on the Romanche. There is an argentiferous lead-mine in the neighbourhood. Pop. 1,546.

ALLEN, a small river in Cornwall, which, uniting with the Kenwyn, falls into a branch of Falmouth harbour.—There is a river of the same name in Flintshire, which disappears for a short distance under ground near Mold, and finally falls into the Dee below Caergwrle. - Also a stream in Dorsetshire, which falls into the Stour near Blandford.-Also two streams, distinguished as the East and the West A., which, rising on the border of Durham, flow N through Northumberland for about 8 m., and uniting between Hexham and Aldstone, fall into the South Tyne about 2 m. above Haydonbridge.—Also a small stream of Scotland in Roxburghshire, flowing S into the | Tweed a little above Melrose.

ALLEN, a township in Northampton co. Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 2,547.—Also a township in Cumberland co. in the same state. Pop. 2,122.—Also a co. of Ohio, on the height of land between Lake Erie and the Ohio. Area 554 sq. m. Pop. 9,079.—Also a co. in the NE of Indiana. Area 650 sq. m. Pop. 5,942. The Wabash and Erie canal passes through this co., of which the cap. is Fort Wayne.—Also a co. on the S border of Kentucky. Pop. 7,329. Capital, Scottsville.—Also a township in Hillsdale co. Michigan. Pop. 466.

ALLEN (Bog of), a large morass, or rather a series of morasses, in the prov. of Leinster, in Ireland, comprising nearly all the morasses N of the Slievebloom mountains, S of the parallel of Mullingar, and E of the Shannon. That portion which lies in the NW of Kildare co. has a superficies of 36,430 acres, with an average elevation of 270 ft. above low tide. The W portion, sometimes called the Barrow bog, is about 41,075 acres in area, with an average elevation of 266 ft.—Near the S extremity of the bog is a cultivated tract of land 42 m. in length, by 3 m. in breadth, known as the Island of A., on the SW of which rises a conical hill of 300 ft. alt. 414.

ALLEN (LOUGH), a lake of Ireland, in the co. of Leitrim and of Roscommon, variously regarded as the source of the Shannon, or as the first of a chain of lakes into which that river expands. It extends N and S 6½ m. Its greatest breadth is 2½ m. On its E side the Slievenerrin and Dowhally mountains rise respectively to the height of 1,400 and 2,000 ft. above sea-level.
ALLEN (SAINT), a parish in Cornwall, 42 m. N by W of Truro. Area 3,610 acres. Pop. 652.
ALLENBURG, a town of East Prussia, in the circle of Königsberg, on the Alle river. Pop. 1,480.
ALLENC, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of the Lozère, cant. of Blaymard. Pop. 1,699.
ALLEN CENTRE, a township in Alleghany co., state of New York, U.S., 7 m. N of Angelica. Pop. 867.

ALLENDALE, a large p. in Northumberland, ecclesiastically divided in 1811 into four rectories and parishes. Area 45,810 acres. Pop. 5,729.—The market town of A. is 93 m. SW by W of Hexham. Pop. 1,217. Houses 221.

ALLENDORF, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, on the Böhr river, 9 m. SSW of Arensberg. Pop. 585.—Also a town of Kur-Hessen, in Lower Hessen, on the Werra, 21 m. W of Muhlhausen. Pop. 3,900. In its vicinity are extensive brine-pits and saltworks.—Also a town of Hessen, on the Lumda, 9 m. NE of Giessen. Pop. 1,100.
ALLENDSTEIG, a town of Austria, on the Manhardsberg, NW of Krems. Pop. 814.
ALLENSBACH, a town of Baden, on the lower Bodensee. Pop. 624.
ALLEN'S ISLE, a low island near the bottom of the gulf of Carpentaria, in Australia; in S lat. 17° 5', E long. 139° 25'.
ALLENSMORE, a p. in Herefordshire, 4 m. SW of Hereford. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 668.
ALLENSTEIN, a town of Prussia, in the gov. of Königsberg, on the Alle river. Pop. 2,787. It has manufactures of potash, glass, linen, and woollen.
ALLENSTOWN, a township in Merrimac co. New Hampshire, U. S., 10 m. SE of Concord. Pop. 445.
ALLENTON, a parish of Northumberland, on the Coquet river, 19 m. W by S of Alnwick. Area of p. 31,940 acres. Pop. 812.
ALLENTOWN, a v. in Monmouth co. New Jersey, U. S., 12 m. E by N of Trenton.-Also the cap. of Lehigh co. in Pennsylvania, 87 m. ENE of Harrisburg. Pop. 2,493. ALL

ALLER, a parish in Somersetshire, 6 m. W of Somerton. Area 4,290 acres. Pop. 559.—Also a river of Germany, which rises to the W of Magdeburg in Prussia, flows NNE into Hanover, and falls into the Weser 2 m. below Verden.
ALLERBY, a village in the p. of Aspatria, in Cumberland, on the line of the Maryport and Carlisle railroad. Pop. with Oughterside 555.

ALLERDALE, a division of Cumberland embracing nearly half the county, and divided into two wards, of which that of A. ABOVE DERWENT has an area of 275,970 acres, with a pop. in 1841 of 41,621; and that of A, BELOW DERWENT of 170,400 acres, with a pop. of 29,896.

ALLERHEILIGEN, a town of Austria, in the circle of the Mühl, to the E of Steyereck.—Also a v. of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, in the vicinity of which was a celebrated Præmonstratensian monastery. Pop. 407.
ALLERSBERG, a town of Bavaria, in the Rezat circle, 16 m. S by E of Nuremberg. Pop. 1,300.
ALLERSHAUSEN, a v. of Bavaria, in the Isar circle, on the Zusammen. Pop. 346.
ALLERSTON, a p. in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. E by S of Pickering. Area 9,110 acres. Pop.
ALLERTHORPE, a p. in the E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 2,050 acres. Pop. 199.—The township of A. is 1½ m. SW by W of Pocklington.—There is a township of the same name 1 m. NE of Wakefield.
ALLERTON, a manufacturing township in the p. of Bradford, W. R. of York, 4 m. WNW of Bradford. Area 1,970 acres. Pop. 1,914.
ALLERTON. See Northallerton.
ALLERTON-CHAPEL, a p. in Somersetshire, 41 m. SW by S of Axbridge. Area 1,490 acres. Pop. 331.—Also a chapelry in the p. of St. Peter, in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 3,040 acres. Pop. 2,580.
ALLERTON - MAULEVERER, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. E by N of Knaresborough. Area 2,300 acres. Pop. 277.

ALLERTONSHIRE, a wapentake in the N. R. of Yorkshire, embracing 14 parishes, or parts of parishes, with an area of 51,500 acres. Pop. in 1831, 9,820; in 1841, 10,025. Inhabited houses in 1831, 1,970; in 1841, 2,063.
ALLERY, a com. of France, in the dep. of the Somme, arrond. of Abbeville. Pop. 951.
ALLESLEY, a parish in Warwickshire, 2 m. N of Coventry. Area 3,950 acres. Pop. 963.
ALLESTAR, or Allistar, a town in the peninsula of Malacca, on the Queda river, inhabited by a mixed race from different parts of India and from China. In 1823, it contained 2,000 houses.

ALLEVARD, a canton and village of France, in the dep. of Isère, on the Ozeins river. The cant., which comprises 6 communes, had a pop. of 8,769 in 1831. The com. and v., which is 23 m. NE of Grenoble, had a pop. in 1842 of 2,690. There are iron works and furnaces in the neighbourhood.
ALLEX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Drome, arrond. of Die. Pop. 1,442.
ALLEXTON, a parish in Leicestershire, 14 m. SE of Leicester, on the Eye, a branch of the Wellund. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 81.
ALLEYRAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, arrond. of Puy. Pop. 880.
ALL HALLOWS, a parish in Cumberland, 6 m. SW of Wigan, on the Ellen river. Area 1,860 acres. Pop. 235.—Also a p. in Kent, 7½ m. NE of Rochester. Area 2,460 acres. Pop. 268.
ALLI, a river of Naples, in Upper Calabria, rising in Mount Calistro, and flowing into the gulf of Squillace, after a SSE course of 25 m.
ALLICHAMP, a com. of France, in the dep. of Haute Marne, arrond. of Vassy, on the Blaise. Pop. | 309.

ALLIER, one of the central dep. of France, composed of the eastern section of the ancient Bourbonnais, and of a small portion of Auvergne; bounded on the NE by the dep. of the Nièvre; on the E by that of the Saone-et-Loire; on the SE by the dep. of the Loire; on the S by that of Puy-de-Dôme; on the W by the dep. of the Creuse; and on the NW by that of the Cher. It is situated between 45° 55' and 46° 48' N lat., and comprises a superficial area of 723,981 hectares. The dep. is intersected from N to S by two low ranges of granitic mountains, whose highest summits do not exceed 2,300 ft., and which separate the basin of the Allier from that of the Loire on the E, and from that of the Cher on the W. The first of these rivers-which gives its name to the dep.-divides it into two nearly equal parts, and receives on the right the Sichon and the Mourgon, and on the left the Andelot, the Sioule, the Queune, the Chamaron, and the Bioudre. The Vouzance, the Odde, the Roudon, and the Bibre, fall into the Loire, which, to a considerable extent, forms the eastern boundary line of the dep. The climate is variable. The winter is often severe, and the summer sometimes extremely warm. The prevailing winds are S and E. The SW wind is in spring very cold. The numerous marshy lakes with which the entire dep. is studded are said to produce malaria. The soil of the level districts is alluvial and generally fertile. About threefifths of the entire area are employed in the culture of grain; about a fortieth in that of the vine; about a third of the latter proportion is occupied with orchards, nurseries, and gardens; an eleventh consists of meadow-land, while more than a thirtieth of the total superficies is still entirely waste. Although A., compared with other districts of France, has made little progress in the arts of agriculture, its production of grain considerably exceeds its consumption; lint and hemp are also extensively cultivated. Besides horses, mules, and asses, to the number of 20,000, about 140,000 cattle and 80,000 sheep are maintained on the fine pasture-lands with which the dep. is adorned. Much attention has been bestowed upon the improvement of the breeds of sheep in this district. The various articles of the dairy produce of Montmerault are held in high reputation. The white wines of the dep. are generally esteemed good, but the red are very inferior. The cultivation of the mulberry and silk-worm-recently much neglected-is again engaging attention, and promises to constitute an important branch of commerce. About two-fifteenths of the surface are covered with wood, comprising varieties of the oak, beech, elm, birch, and fir, and affording shelter to wolves, foxes, polecats, &c. The wild boar, formerly very common, is now rarely seen. Game of all kinds is plentiful; and fish abounds in all the rivers and lakes. Iron, copper, antimony, manganese, granite, porphyry, marble—of the finest description—freestone, quartz, potter's-clay, lime, and coal, form the mineral riches. The mineral springs have long been celebrated; those of Néris, Bourbonl'Archambault, and Vichy, are the most frequented. The manufacture of iron constitutes one of the most important branches of trade in this dep. The forges of Tronçais alone produce the annual quantity of 500,000 kilogrammes, or 1,102,500 lbs. of iron. There are also a paper-mill at Cusset, glass-works at Souvigny and Commeutry, manufactures of cutlery at Moulins, of pottery and porcelain at Lurcy-Levy, of quilts and of cloths of various kinds, spinning-mills, paper-mills, tanneries, rope-works, &c. Fairs to the number of 406, and generally lasting only one day, are held in 92 of the communes, the trade of which consists principally in grain, dried pulse, hemp, lint, ALL cattle, pottery, cutlery, &c .- The dep. of A. is divided into four arrondissements, named after their principal towns, Moulins, Montluçon, Gannat, and Palisse; and was subdivided in 1836 into 25 cantons and 322 communes. It belongs to the 19th military division, and the 23d forest arrond.; and forms the diocese of a suffragan of the archbishopric of Sens. Besides the three principal rivers by which it is intersected, it is navigated by the canals of the Loire and of the Cher, and is traversed by nine royal roads and seven belonging to the dep. Pop. in 1836, 309,270, of whom 41,247 belonged to towns with a pop. exceeding 3,000, viz., Moulins, Cusset, Arfeuilles, Ferrières, Gannat, Saint Pourçain, and Montluçon. In 1801, the pop. was 248,864.

ALLIER, a considerable river of France, which rises near the hamlet of Chabalier, in the forest of Mercoire, on Mont Lozère, 18 m. NE of Mende, at an alt. of 4,668 ft. above sea-level; and flows in a N by W direction, through the dep. of Lozère, the Haute- Loire, Puy-de-Dome, Allier, and Cher, in the latter of which it joins the Loire, on the l. bank, at Becd'Allier, 3 m. below Nevers, at an alt. of 550 ft. above sea-level, and after a course of 250 m., of which 96 m., from Saint-Arcons to Mariol, are fit for the transport of timber, and 87 m., from Mariol to the Loire, are navigable. Coal, charcoal, and wine, form the chief articles of transport on this river. Its principal tributary on the r. is the Dore; and on the l. the Sioule.

ALLIGATOR, a river of North Australia, flowing into Van Diemen's gulf, in S lat. 12° 20', by several large mouths, one of which has 8 fath. water for 9 m. up, and shoals gradually to 15 ft. at 36 m. from its mouth, where it is 150 yards in breadth.—King.

ALLIGATOR BAY, an inlet at the SW end of the isle of Floris, in the Indian archipelago.—Also a bay on the S coast of Jamaica, in which is a kay or low islet of the same name, rising just above the surface of the water, and seated on a reef which extends 2 m. in an E and W direction.

ALLIGATOR POINT, a headland on the western coast of Africa, between Tumbo point and the mouth of the Sangaree river, forming the W extremity of the Soomba ridge of mountains.—Also a cape on the W coast of Florida, in N lat. 29° 20'.
ALLIGATOR SWAMP, a marsh in North Carolina, U. S., between Albemarle and Pamlico sounds, and to the S of the great Dismal swamp. It gives rise to a small stream of the same name.
ALLIGNY, a com. and town of France, in the dep. of Nievre, 14 m. NE of Chateau-Chinon. Pop. 2,361.—Also a com. and village in the same dep., in the arrond. of Cosne. Pop. 1,746.
ALLINENC, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Loudeac. Pop. 2,533.
ALLINGE, a port of Denmark, on the NE coast of the island of Bornholm. Pop. 350.

ALLINGTON, a parish in Dorsetshire, adjoining Bridport. Area 960 acres. Pop. 1,545.—Also a p. in Kent, on the Medway. Area 530 acres. Pop. 49. A castle, one of the most perfect remains of the feudal age in the co., is 11 m. NNW of Maidstone.— Also a p. in Wiltshire, 3 m. ESE of Amesbury. Area 460 acres. Pop. 94.—Also a tything in the p. of Allcannings, Wilts; 4 m. ENE of Devizes. Pop. 188.
ALLINGTON (East), a parish in Devonshire, 32 m. NE of Kingsbridge. Area 4,226 acres. Pop. 729. -Also a p. in Lincolnshire. Pop. 276.
ALLINGTON (West), a parish in Devonshire, 1 m. WSW of Kingsbridge. Area 3,840 acres. Pop. 998.—Also a p. in Lincolnshire, 51 m. NW of Grantham. Area, with that of E A., 2,070 acres. Pop. 120.
ALLINGTON (CAPE), a rugged point on the W coast of the gulf of Boothia, forming the N boundary of Eclipse harbour.—Ross
ALLISON (Point), a cape on the NW coast of Chatham island, in the South Pacific, 15 m. WSW from Cape Young; in S lat. 43° 42'.
ALLISON'S BAY, an inlet on the coast of Baffin's bay; in N lat. 74° 42′, W long. 57° 50′.

ALLITHWAITE (Lower and Upper), two townships in Lancashire, in the p. of Cartmell; the former with a pop. of 807, and an area of 2,360 acres; the latter with a pop. of 740, and an area of 3,710 acres.

ALLMANNS, a name sometimes given to a chain of mountains in Switzerland, which traverse the cantons of Zurich, and skirt those of Saint-Gall and Thurgau; and in which the Horndly attains an alt. of 3,824 ft. above sea-level.
ALLMANNSDORF, a village of Baden, 3 m. NE of Constanz, on the shore of the lake, opposite the islet of Mainau. Pop. 683.
ALLMANSWEILER, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, 3 m. ENE of Saulgau. Pop. 250.
ALLMANSWEYER, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, 6 m. NW of Lahr. Pop. 625.

ALLOA, a parish and port on the N coast of the 1,053. frith of Forth, in the shire of Clackmannan. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 5,214; in 1841, 7,921, of whom 2,158 formed the pop. of the town of A., which is 7 m. below Stirling, and 271 m. above Leith; in W long. 3° 46', N lat. 56° 7'. The arrivals and sailings at this port in 1846 were 439 vessels of 31,940 tons, of which 78 vessels were to or from the colonies or foreign; and the customs duty was £1,859. Coals form the great article of exportation. It has frequently been proposed to build a bridge across the Forth at this point, which may be regarded as the lowest point of the river across which a bridge is practicable.
ALLONBY, a fishing-village in Cumberland, on a small bay of the same name, opening to the Solway frith and the Irish sea; 9 m. NNW of Cockermouth. Pop. 811.
ALLONE, a village of France, in the dep. of Eureet-Loir, arrond. of Chartres, 3 m. NNE of Voves. Pop. 416.
ALLONES, a town of Spain, in Gallicia, at the mouth of a river of the same name, which forms a large estuary in N lat. 43° 14'.
ALLONNE, a com. and village of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, arrond. of Parthenay. Pop. 1,522.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Beauvais. Pop. 1,403.
ALLONNES, a com. and town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, arrond. of Saumur. Pop. 2,049.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Sarthe, arrond. of Le Mans. Pop. 725.
ALLONS, a com. and village of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes, arrond. of Castellanne. Pop. 488.
ALLOS, a town of France, in the dep. of Basses- Alpes, arrond. and 10 m. S of Barcelonnette. Pop. 1,313.
ALLOSTOCK, a township in the p. of Great Budworth, Cheshire, 5 m. S by W of Nether Knutsford. Area 2,540 acres. Pop. 427.
ALLOUARN ISLANDS (SAINT), two rocky bar- Acre. ren islets, 3 or 4 m. SE from Cape Leeuwin, the SW extremity of New Holland.
ALLOUAGNE, a com. and village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Bethune. Pop. 1,111.
ALLOUE', a com. and village of France, in the dep. of Charente, arrond. of Confolens. Pop. 1,659.
ALLOUVILLE-BELLEFOSSE, a com. and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Yvetot. Pop. 1,239.

ALLOW, a stream in the county of Durham, flowing into the Tyne near Gateshead, and distinguished by the appellation East, from that of another stream | a boundary between the pashaliks of Marash and ALM called the West A., in Northumberland, which joins the Tyne between Hexham and Newcastle.-There is a stream of the same name in Anglesea.
ALLOWALLA, a district of India, in the NE of the prov. of Delhi.

ALLOWAY, or ALLOWAY-KIRK, a village in Ayrshire, on the E bank of the Doon, 13 m. from Ayr. Burns, the peasant-bard of Scotland, was born in the vicinity of this v., on the 25th of January, 1759.— Also a village in Wayne co. New York, U. S., 183 m. WNW from Albany. Pop. 300.
ALLOWAY'S CREEK, an affluent of the Delaware, rising in Salem co. New Jersey, U. S., and fallinto that river 6 m. S of Salem, after a course of 20 m., 12 of which are navigable for wood-boats.
ALL SAINTS, the name of a number of parishes in different cities and towns of England; and of a few rural parishes, which are generally distinguished by an additional appellative.
ALL SAINTS. See Todos Santos.
ALLSCHWEILER, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Basle, on the frontier of France. Pop.
ALLSTADT, or Allstedt, a district and village of Saxe-Weimar, on the Rohne, 10 m. NNW of Querfert. Pop. 2,470.—Also a town of West Prussia, 8 m. SE of Lobau.
ALLUES (LES), a village of Sardinia, in the district of Bozel, 3 m. SSE of Moutiers.
ALLUFATI, one of the Fiji isles, NE of Tacanova, in S lat. 14°.

ALLUMETTES (Lac-des), a lake-like expansion of the Ottawa river, in Canada, immediately below the island Des Allumettes, or Black river island. The river is here 10 m. in length, by 2 m. or 3 m. in breadth, and after contracting into a narrow rapid for a mile, it again expands into another lake of the same name.
ALLUMPARVA, a town of India, in the Carnatic, 25 m. N by E from Pondicherry.
ALLUY, a com. and village of France, in the dep. of Nievre, arrond. of Chateau-Chinon. Pop. 1,255.
ALLY, a com. and village of France, in the dep. of Cantal, arrond. of Mauriac. Pop. 1,190.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Haute-Loire, arrond. of Brioude. Pop. 860.
ALLY-MOHUN, a district of India, in the prov. of Malwah, about 12 m. N of the Nerbudda.

ALLYN, a river of New South Wales, in Durham co., descending from a range of hills which attain an elevation of from 3,000 to 4,000 ft., and flowing in a S by E course into the Hunter river on its l. bank.
ALM, a small river of Austria, an affluent of the Traun, which it joins on the r. bank, SW of Wels.

ALMA, a river of Russia, in the Crimea, rising in the western declivity of the Chatyrdagh, and flowing in a W by N but meandering course, through an open fertile country, into the Black sea between Acmetchet and Baktschisarai.—There is a river of the same name in Kamtschatka.—Also a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Acre, 12 m. N by E of
ALMACEN-DEL-REY, a port of Peru, at the mouth of the Chincha river, 115 m. S by E of Lima.
ALMACHAR, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 11 m. NE of Malaga.

ALMADA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, on the l. bank of the estuary of the Tagus, nearly opposite Lisbon. Pop. 5,000. Alt. above sea-level 280 ft. It is an entrepot for the exportation of wines.

ALMADAGH, a mountain-range of Asiatic Turkey, branching off in a SW direction from Mount Taurus towards the gulf of Iskenderun, and forming Aleppo, or between Cilicia and Syria, which is penetrated by two narrow passes, the one towards the Euphrates, called the Almanian defiles; the other close by the sea, and known as the Gates of Syria.

ALMADEN, or ALMADEN-DEL-AZOGUE, [i.e. 'the Mine of Quicksilver,'] a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the district of La Mancha, 59 m. N of Cordova. It consists chiefly of one long narrow street, built on a scarped ridge of quartz rock, which is rich in cinnabar; and the inhabitants, 8,000 in number, are chiefly supported by the profits of the quicksilver workings which exist here, and are supposed to have been known in Roman times. The mines are a royal monopoly, and were for a long series of ages wrought only by criminals. The chain-labour system has been abolished within the last 40 years, and the mines now furnish employment to about 5,000 free hands of all ages; and experience having shown that the labour in the mines can be pursued with less danger in the spring and winter months than during the heat of summer and autumn, all engaged are freely allowed to quit the mines, and to occupy themselves as long as they please in agricultural or other labour. The interior of the mine resembles a series of streets more than veins, many of the passages being 14 ft. wide, and several hundred feet in length. The ventilation is consequently pretty good; but the quantity of mercurial vapour which is at all times disseminated throughout the workings, renders the employment of a miner here very unhealthful. The mines have now attained a depth of about 300 varas, or nearly 800 ft., in the 7th or deepest level, but the ore presents no alteration in quality, or diminution in quantity; and notwithstanding all difficulties, political, pecuniary, sanatory, mechanical, chemical, and incendiary-for the mines have been on fire for 30 months together, when convicts were compelled to labour in, and permitted to burn them—their produce has been steadily increasing ever since they have been worked on account of the Spanish treasury. Previous to 1646—the date on which the Ministro de Hacienda took them into his hands—they had been rented to the celebrated and wealthy German house of Fuggars, who pursued their works in a ruinous style, blasting the richest ores only, and leaving the roofs unsupported to fall in at leisure. Since that period, however, a safer system has been adopted; and the produce, which only averaged 2,500 quintals in the years intervening between 1646 and 1700, has now risen to 22,000 quintals. It was calculated in 1839, when the produce had attained to 20,500 quintals, that during the 193 years these mines had been conducted on account of the Spanish government, there had been £55,000,000 sterling worth of silver, coined and uncoined, brought into circulation and into the markets of Europe by the instrumentality of the mercury supplied from this mine. The value of the mercury obtained from Almaden, in 1842, as stated by the minister of finance in his estimates, amounted nearly to 25,000,000 reals, delivered to the contractors at the price of <math>54\frac{1}{4}</math> dollars per quintal. But of this sum something more than one-fourth is returned to pay the costs of production; so that not much more than 18,000,000 reals (about £180,000) are available in the shape of revenue from this peculiar source to the government. The cost of working the mines of A. is certainly much greater than it would be if improved machinery were employed; but improvements of any kind find their way slowly into Spain, especially into remote mountain-districts devoid of roads and infested with bands of robbers. The labour of draining the mine is severe and expensive. A grand reservoir has been formed in the fifth level, and into this the water of the lower levels is elevated by hand-pumps, at an expense of manual labour of 300,000 reals per annum: although ALM the elevation of this general receptacle above the greatest depth is only 110 varas. It is then pumped to the surface by a single-stroke steam-engine, the annual cost of which is reckoned at £600 sterling. Besides these motive powers, steam and manual, from 35 to 40 mules are kept constantly at work,-eight at once,-in drawing up the ore by a rude wheel capstan, the friction of which is so great that the animals can only work three hours in the 24. There is no water-power available to move machinery in these mines; but little effort has been made to render any of the mechanical powers available for the abridgment of labour. Even the ore, which is brought up from the deep sinkings by mules at the rate of 300,500 arrobas (25 lb. each) per day of 12 hours, is drawn to the furnaces by oxen, in rude carros, without the slightest aid from a railway; and notwithstanding that the underground works had been going on for ages, it was only in 1802 that any forge for sharpening tools was fitted up in the mine.
ALMADEN-DE-LA-PLATA, a town of Spain, in Seville, on the Colar river, 32 m. N of Seville. celebrated, like the above, for its quicksilver mines. Pop. 1,100.

ALMADEN (New). See California, p. 194. ALMADIES, "a flat of blackish rocks awash with the water's edge," and in a few places rising from 8 to 10 feet above the sea, forming the extreme eastern point of Cape Verde, the westernmost point of Africa. The highest and westernmost of these rocks is in N lat. 14° 44′, W long. 17° 35′. The point in which they join the mainland is called Almadia Point.
ALMADRONES, a town of Spain, in New Castile, 25 m. NE of Guadalaxara.—Also a v. of Marocco, midway between Cape Spartel and Arzilla.
ALMAGREIRA, a town of Portugal, in Beira, on the road between Coimbra and Leiria, 20 m. SW of the former place.

ALMAGRERA (SIERRA DE), a ridge of hills between Almazarron and Cartagena in Murcia, in which, in April 1839, a rich lode of galena or argentiferous lead was discovered, which is now wrought to great advantage by a joint-stock company.

ALMAGRO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 13 m. SE by E of Ciudad-Real. It conducts a considerable traffic in wines, asses, mules, and agricultural produce. Pop. 8,000. There is a fine convent here.

ALMAGUER, a town of Ecuador, 50 m. S by W of Popayan, on a plateau of the Andes of Quito, at an alt. of 8,030 ft. above sea-level. It enjoys a mild and pleasant temperature. There are some goldworkings in the neighbourhood.

ALMALE, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Tekieh, 42 m. SW of Adalia, at the NE end of an extensive plain. It contains about 1,500 houses, and has several mosques, and a baazar. It is resorted to by Frank merchants, whose purchases here are generally sent to Makri for exportation.—Also a town of European Turkey, 26 m. N by E of Gallipoli.
ALMALIYAH, a large village of Kurdistan, belonging to Bitlis, near Lake Van. It is in a level district, and is surrounded by orchards.
ALMAN, a river of South America, rising in the Andes, and flowing into the sea opposite Chiloe isle, in S lat. 42° 32'.

ALMANSA, a town of Spain, in Murcia, 60 m. ESE of Albacete. Pop. 6,000. Near this town, on the 25th of April, 1707, the French, commanded by the duke of Berwick, a natural son of James II., defeated the allied forces under Ruvigny, earl of Galway.—There is another town of the same name in the prov. of Leon, on the l. bank of the Coa.

ALMANSOR, a river of Portugal rising in Alemtejo, and after being joined by the Cantra, entering $ Estremadura, and falling into the Tagus, 1 m. below Samora Corvera, after a course of 20 m. from SE to NW.
ALMANSORA, a river of Spain, in Andalusia, rising in the Sierra Nevada, and flowing into the sea a little to the SW of Point Villa Ricos, after a course from W to E of about 36 m.

ALMARAZ, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, near the r. bank of the Tagus, on the road from Truxillo to Talavera-de-la-Reina. Pop. 1,000. The Tagus is here crossed by a magnificent bridge, built in 1552 at the expense of the city of Placencia to open a communication with La Mancha. Lord Hill took his Spanish title from this place where, on the 18th of May, 1812, he achieved "with consummate ability one of the most brilliant actions in the war," in the surprise and destruction of the French works at A.
ALMARS, a town of Sweden, on the S side of a small lake formed by the Nuirunda river, 12 m. SW of Sundswall.

ALMAS, a town of Austria, near the confluence of the Drave with the Danube.—Also a town of Hungary, on the r. bank of the Danube, 8 m. E of Comorn. -There are several villages in Transylvania which bear this name with some adjunct, such as Kis or Nagy.—Also a river of Brazil, an affluent of the Toccantines, in the prov. of Goyaz.

ALMAS (Montes de Las), a mountain-range in Brazil, a continuation of the Serra de Manguira, running from N to S through the prov. of Bahia and Minas Geraes, and in which the Rio Pardo, the Rio Grande, and the Rio Verde have their sources.
ALMAZAN, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. of Soria, on the l. bank of the Douro, 16 m. S of Soria. Pop. 2,000.

ALMAZARRON, a town of Spain, in Murcia, near the coast, 21 m. W of Cartagena. Pop. 5,000. Silver ore is wrought in the adjacent Sierra, and alumand almagra—a kind of red earth used for polishing mirrors and other purposes—are extracted from the adjacent hill of San Cristobal.
ALMAZORA, a village of Spain, in Valencia, 2 m. SSE of Castellon-de-la-Plana, on the Mijares, celebrated for its manufacture of a highly spiced kind of sausage.
ALME, a river of Prussia, in Westphalia, flowing into the Lippe, 2 m. WNW of Paderborn.—There are two villages of the same name, but distinguished as UPPER and on this stream.

ALMEIDA (VILLA NOVA DE), a village of Brazil, founded by the Jesuits on elevated ground near the mouth of the Rio dos Reys Magos, 12 m. N of Espiritu Santo. The inhabitants of the v. and district, 1,200 in number, are chiefly Indians, who support themselves by their plantations of mandioc and maize, and by fishing on the coast and in the river.-Also a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, on the frontier of Bolivia.

ALMEIDA, a town and frontier fortress of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, on the Coa river, 213 m. NE of Lisbon. Pop. 1,200. Its citadel has six bastions, is flanked by wide trenches, and contains bombproof magazines. On the 25th of August, 1762, it capitulated, after an heroic resistance, to Count O'Reilly, at the head of 40,000 Spaniards and French. In the following year it was restored to Portugal. In 1810 it was captured by the French, favoured by the accidental explosion of the powder magazine; but in the following year it was evacuated by its captors after the action at Fuentes d'Onoro. The surrounding region is bare, and fertile only in acorns.
ALMEIDA BAY, a safe anchorage in the Mozambique channel, sheltered by the shoals of Mamabala and Indujo. ALM
ALMELEY, a parish in Herefordshire, 54m. W of Weobley. Area 3,630 acres. Pop. 642.
